verses.text: David organizes his forces and would lead them himself, but the people insist he stay back — he is worth ten thousand of them. Wisdom sometimes keeps a leader from the front line. The king yields to their counsel, valuing the people's care over his own wish to fight.

Before the battle David charges his commanders, in all the army's hearing, 'Deal gently for my sake with the young man, even with Absalom.' The father's love overrides the king's justice. Even toward a rebel son, David's heart pleads for mercy.

The battle spreads through the wood of Ephraim, and twenty thousand fall, the forest itself devouring more than the sword. Rebellion against God's anointed ends in slaughter, and the very ground seems to fight against it. Sin's war reaps a terrible harvest.

Fleeing on his mule, Absalom is caught fast by his head in the boughs of a great oak, hanging helpless between heaven and earth. The glory of his hair becomes his snare. The very thing men prize in us can be the trap that holds us when judgment comes.

Fleeing on his mule, Absalom is caught fast by his head in the boughs of a great oak, hanging helpless between heaven and earth. The glory of his hair becomes his snare. The very thing men prize in us can be the trap that holds us when judgment comes.

Joab rebukes the man who saw Absalom and would not strike him; but the soldier answers he would not lift a hand against the king's son for any reward, having heard David's charge. One ordinary man honours the king's word where the hardened general will not.

Joab takes three darts and thrusts them through Absalom's heart, and his armour-bearers finish the killing. He defies David's plain command to spare the boy. Joab serves the kingdom's interest as he sees it, but tramples the king's expressed will to do it.

Joab takes three darts and thrusts them through Absalom's heart, and his armour-bearers finish the killing. He defies David's plain command to spare the boy. Joab serves the kingdom's interest as he sees it, but tramples the king's expressed will to do it.

Joab sounds the retreat to spare the people, and Absalom is thrown into a pit and covered with stones. The man who built himself a monument to keep his name alive ends under a heap of rubble. Pride's grand memorials cannot outrun the grave it earns.

Ahimaaz begs to run with the news, and though warned, outruns the Cushite messenger. He is eager to carry tidings — but, as it turns out, not the hard part of them. Zeal to bring good news is easy; it is the bearing of sorrow that tests a messenger.

David sits between the gates, and the watchman reports first one runner, then another. The king's eyes are not on the victory but on word of his son. A father's anxious heart waits, dreading the news it most needs to hear. Love makes the watching unbearable.

Ahimaaz arrives crying 'All is well,' but when David asks of Absalom, he dodges — he saw a tumult but knows not what. He cannot bring himself to speak the death. Sometimes good news must wait while the heavy truth is left for another to tell.

The Cushite tells it plainly, and David breaks: 'O my son Absalom, my son, my son! would God I had died for thee.' A father longs to die in his rebel son's place. Here is a faint shadow of the greater love that did die for rebels — the Son given for us.
